questions.question: Why are time-series systems often modeled around time-bounded queries instead of arbitrary filtering?
options: Because the main reads usually ask for a metric over a defined recent or historical time window.
Because time-series systems never support retention policies.
Because appends remove the need for partitioning.
Because dashboard users do not care about latency.
questions.explanation: Bounded time windows are the common serving pattern and should shape the storage layout.

questions.question: What is the main operational advantage of append-heavy writes?
options: They naturally align with sequential ingest paths and avoid many random update patterns.
They guarantee query costs remain low at any lookback window.
They remove the need for retention planning.
They make cardinality irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Append-heavy ingest can be efficient, but it still needs partitioning and query discipline.

questions.question: Why are rollups useful in time-series systems?
options: They make long-range queries cheaper and reduce storage pressure for old raw data.
They guarantee no late-arriving data will ever be dropped.
They eliminate the need for recent hot storage.
They make every query strongly consistent with the latest point.
questions.explanation: Rollups trade precision for cheaper long-range queries and lower retention cost.

questions.question: Which metric best detects a dangerous recent-partition hotspot?
options: Partition skew or queue depth concentrated in current buckets.
Only total disk size across the cluster.
The number of documentation pages for the service.
Median dashboard color theme preference.
questions.explanation: Recent-bucket skew reveals when one tenant or metric family is overloading the hot ingest path.

questions.question: What is the hidden cost of letting users run unbounded time-range queries on the serving path?
options: They can scan far more data than the interactive system is sized to handle.
They disable data compression automatically.
They prevent retention jobs from running at all.
They remove the need for caching.
questions.explanation: Large lookback scans can overwhelm the cluster and starve interactive traffic.

questions.question: How should a strong design treat late-arriving time-series data?
options: Ignore it because append-heavy systems should never allow disorder.
Define a clear acceptance window and repair or reconciliation policy.
Force all writes into one global serial order.
Store it only in the archive tier.
questions.explanation: Late data handling is a policy choice that affects correctness and operational cost.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping raw points for longer?
options: Higher fidelity and replay value, but more storage and slower long-range queries unless tiered.
Instantly better cardinality behavior without extra cost.
Less need for query limits on dashboards.
Automatic elimination of aggregation lag.
questions.explanation: Raw retention preserves detail, but it is expensive unless paired with good lifecycle controls.

questions.question: Which interview answer is strongest?
options: I will build one large append table and trust the database to optimize the rest.
I will partition by time, bound query windows, and use rollups or archives for long-range access.
I will avoid retention to keep the model simple.
I will store dashboards directly as blobs instead of time-series points.
questions.explanation: That answer connects ingest shape, query shape, and lifecycle management coherently.
